what_it_does: Defines an autonomous, cron-driven remediation build loop for the Company repo: it reads a Master Remediation Spec, dispatches parallel file-disjoint lanes (BACKEND, TESTS, DATA-HYGIENE, VERIFY) on a remediation branch, verifies each fix by driving the operator's real end-to-end path across layers, commits per criterion, and flags or blocks what needs human attention, while explicitly excluding front-end work.
observations: It is a methodology/runbook document with YAML frontmatter, not executable code.
It creates a `remediation` branch off `main`, uses a gitignored `.build/remediation/` state directory, and coordinates via `state.json`, per-lane `report.json` files, and a timestamped `loop.lock`.
It splits work into four file-disjoint lanes so BACKEND, TESTS, and DATA-HYGIENE can run in parallel, with VERIFY running only after BACKEND lands.
The front-end / FORM / `canvas/app/**` territory is deliberately out of scope for this loop.
It mandates verification by USE at the seam (real operator-facing end-to-end path), not by reading code or running isolated units.
It distinguishes buildable (codeable here) from closeable (verifiable here) criteria, and marks backend-only halves of FE-paired criteria as `needs-tim`.
It hard-blocks `T3-SETTINGS` and forbids touching `panels/settings.json` in this loop to avoid shipping a half-fix regression.
It splits `T0-WIRE` (owned by BACKEND, one good seam drive) from `T2-WIRE-YOUNG` (owned by VERIFY, reliability battery including a breaking run).
It requires per-criterion commits with a specific message format and a Co-Authored-By trailer.
It ends by writing a `WALKTHROUGH.md` and notifying Tim when all criteria are `verified`, `blocked`, or `needs-tim`.
standouts: The central spine: verify the SEAM, not the CELL.
The hard guard on `T3-SETTINGS` — deliberately not fixing a known defect in this loop because the FE half is missing.
The buildable-vs-closeable split-criterion rule for criteria that span backend and front-end.
Parallel BACKEND/TESTS/DATA-HYGIENE with VERIFY last, enabled by file-disjoint territories.
conventions: Markdown methodology file with YAML frontmatter naming and describing the loop.
Branch-based isolation (`remediation` off `main`) plus gitignored local state.
Single-writer per-lane JSON reports and a stale-lock reclamation mechanism.
Per-criterion commits with descriptive messages and a Co-Authored-By trailer.
Heavy referencing of an external Master Remediation Spec by section and criterion ID.
Worker prompts must read `AGENTS.md`, `MAP.md`, `STATE.md`, and module `AGENTS.md` first.
concerns: The actual criteria definitions, file map, and completion criteria live in an external spec not shown here, so this file is not self-contained.
The parallel agent dispatch and `mode auto` execution model are assumed but not defined in this file.
Several hard guards depend on worker discipline rather than enforced technical boundaries.
The verification step relies on external services/routes (`bridge.py :8770`, `mcp_face/server.py`, BGE-M3 :8001) whose contracts are not detailed here.
notes: This is the operational playbook for an autonomous backend/test/data remediation loop that fixes cross-layer defects by proving them through real end-to-end use while leaving all front-end changes for a separate FORM session.
questions: What are the exact definitions and acceptance conditions for each spec §3 criterion ID (T0-WIRE, T1-EMIT, etc.)?
What is the schema and current content of the Master Remediation Spec file?
How are the parallel Agent workers instantiated, and what does `mode auto` mean in this context?
What is `drift_acceptance.py` and where is it located?
What are the contents and schemas of `AGENTS.md`, `MAP.md`, and `STATE.md`?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose — an autonomous cross-layer defect remediation loop — matches the detailed operational procedure; because it is a methodology document rather than executable code, actual execution depends on external agents, spec, and repo state.
apparent_themes: Cross-layer integration over isolated unit correctness.
Safety through file-disjoint parallelism and explicit scope exclusion.
Verification by real operator-facing use at seams.
Fail-loud behavior, additive schema changes, and registry-as-source-of-truth.
Human-in-the-loop for non-headless or front-end-paired decisions.
intention_signals: It is trying to eliminate the recurring bug class where backend fixes are technically correct but still broken or unreachable from the operator's real end-to-end path, by forcing every fix to be proven at the seam and by keeping front-end work in a separate, deliberate session.
novelty: The explicit buildable-but-not-closeable split for backend/front-end paired criteria, and the unusual hard guard that intentionally leaves a known settings defect untouched in this loop to avoid regressing the live panel.
contribution: A bounded remediation protocol that separates backend/test/data fixes from front-end work, mandates seam-level end-to-end verification, and provides state/lock/report conventions for coordinating parallel autonomous workers.
summary_for_embedding: A methodology document defining a cron-driven, parallel remediation build loop for cross-layer backend, test, and data-hygiene defects in the Company repo, with end-to-end seam verification and explicit exclusion of front-end changes.
intention_for_embedding: To provide an autonomous, crash-safe remediation procedure that closes backend and test criteria by proving them through real operator-facing end-to-end use while deferring front-end changes to a separate session.
